query: Angular hierarchical dependency injection real bug interview question

competitors.label: Angular Hierarchical Injectors Guide
competitors.selectionReason: This is the official Angular guide for hierarchical DI, making it the canonical competitor for injector tree behavior and provider scope.
competitors.winReason: Angular docs are the canonical DI reference, but our page is more immediately useful for the specific production failure mode: silently splitting state by re-providing a service lower in the injector tree.
theirStrengths: It is the official explanation of injector hierarchy and provider scope.
theirGaps: It is less bug-first than our focused answer about broken singleton assumptions.

realWorldUseCases: that becomes a production bug when a root service is accidentally re-provided in a component or route and your “singleton” cache, auth flag, or shared state quietly splits into multiple instances.
actionableExamples: Standalone route providers are their own injector boundary
followUpConfusion: 1) Check the component’s injector 2) If not found, check parent component injectors 3) Then module/route injectors 4) Finally root injector
In modern Angular, route-level providers create an EnvironmentInjector for that branch.

realWorldUseCases: The official guide explains hierarchical injectors clearly, but our page puts the broken-singleton bug front and center instead of presenting hierarchy more abstractly.
actionableExamples: Angular.dev is still strong enough on provider placement and injector behavior that the example comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: The docs explain scope and hierarchy, but our page is more direct about the first-match-wins rule and lazy-module split-state bug.

actionableExamples: Add one full before-versus-after snippet that shows the same token resolved inside and outside the route subtree if we want to beat the official DI guide on example explicitness.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Angular Basic Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd directly covers Angular services, provider scope, and dependency injection as interview topics, so it is the right interview-prep comparator.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd covers DI and service scope basics well, but our page is stronger on the concrete bug pattern where a re-provided service silently splits shared state.
theirStrengths: It introduces services, provider scope, and DI in interview-friendly language.
theirGaps: It does not focus as strongly on the broken-singleton production bug and lazy-route split-state behavior.

realWorldUseCases: The usual answer: you accidentally created multiple instances of the same service.
actionableExamples: Standalone route providers are their own injector boundary
followUpConfusion: In modern Angular, route-level providers create an EnvironmentInjector for that branch.

realWorldUseCases: The basic guide explains provider scope, but our page anchors the concept in cache resets, auth-state splits, and other real failure modes.
actionableExamples: Its service-scope examples keep the example comparison close.
followUpConfusion: It is lighter on the full injector-resolution path and why lazy-loaded modules can create a second instance.

actionableExamples: Add one even more compact root-versus-route provider diff if we want to beat an interview-prep page on glanceable example density.

competitors.label: Angular University Dependency Injection Guide
competitors.selectionReason: Angular University has a dedicated DI guide, making it the strongest tutorial/reference competitor for injector mechanics and provider patterns.
competitors.winReason: Angular University is still the fuller DI tutorial, but our page now wins the specific route-provider follow-up: it shows the same token resolving differently at the root and route level and explains standalone route EnvironmentInjector behavior more directly.
theirStrengths: It is a focused long-form DI tutorial with more room for provider mechanics and examples.
theirGaps: It is less compact and less narrowly shaped around a bug-first interview answer.

actionableExamples: Standalone route providers are their own injector boundary
followUpConfusion: In modern Angular, route-level providers create an EnvironmentInjector for that branch.

realWorldUseCases: Angular University also ties DI to practical app architecture and provider placement, so the real-world comparison stays close.
actionableExamples: It walks through more total DI mechanics, but our updated page now shows the exact root-versus-route split with less indirection.
followUpConfusion: It covers injector behavior in depth, but our page now answers the standalone route-provider EnvironmentInjector follow-up more explicitly.

realWorldUseCases: Add one explicit note on how route-level providers differ from component providers in standalone Angular.
